title: How embodiment works
subtitle: The physical anchor — hand on heart, hand on belly, palms up — that turns a sentence into a practice.
excerpt: A reference article for the embodiment practices in the library. Covers what embodiment is in this context, the four physical anchors used across the scripts, why a body posture changes what a sentence does, and how to use embodiment alongside affirmations or energy statements. Read once; the practice scripts assume it.
sourceNotes: Embodiment as practice draws on somatic therapy traditions (Peter Levine's Somatic Experiencing, Pat Ogden's Sensorimotor Psychotherapy), polyvagal theory (Stephen Porges, 1994 onward), and the body-based mindfulness lineage. The pairing of a specific posture with a spoken sentence is documented across these traditions. The practical anchors used in the library are drawn from across Rebecca J Page's MONEY (2025) and MANIFESTING (2025) programs.

practice.whenToUse: Read once before using any embodiment practice in the library. Re-read if a particular hand placement feels unfamiliar or you're not sure why a script pairs a specific posture with a specific sentence.

content.text: Embodiment practices pair a specific physical anchor with a specific sentence. Hand on heart, "You were doing your best." Feet flat on the floor, shoulders down, "I am allowed to take up the space I take up." Palms turned up on the thighs, "I am open to receive." The posture is the body's part of the work; the sentence is the mind's part. Both are required.

This reading covers what embodiment is here, the four physical anchors used across the scripts, why a body posture changes what a sentence does, and how to use embodiment alongside affirmations or energy statements. The individual practice scripts assume it.

A sentence said with the shoulders rounded forward, jaw clenched, breath shallow, lands differently from the same sentence said with feet flat on the floor, hand on heart, breath low and even. The body sends the nervous system information about whether the sentence is safe to take in. Posture is part of how the sentence does its work.

This is why "I am safe with money" said while pacing and refreshing the banking app lands differently from "I am safe with money" said with the phone away, feet flat, hand on the centre of the chest. The sentence is the same; the body's information about it isn't.
The four anchors used in the library

Right hand flat on the centre of the chest, fingers gently spread. The most widely-used anchor in the library. Signals self-compassion. Used with sentences of forgiveness, acknowledgement, or care directed at yourself — "You were doing your best." "I am allowed to want this." "It is okay to be where you are."

Right hand flat on the lower belly, two fingers below the navel. Signals settling and groundedness. Used with sentences that ask the body to soften or trust — "I am held by this moment." "My body knows how to do this." Pairs well with a slow breath into the hand.

Both feet flat on the floor, palms turned up resting on the thighs. Signals openness and receiving. Used with sentences about welcoming, opening, or letting in — "I am open to receive." "I let the good come in." "Help is welcome here."

Both hands flat on the chest, one stacked on the other. Signals weight and presence. Used with sentences about commitment, intention, or owning something — "This is mine." "I am here." "I choose this." Heavier than the single-hand version; for sentences that need to land with weight.
How to use embodiment alongside other practices

Embodiment lifts the other practices. Take any affirmation in the library and say it with a hand on heart instead of just in your head; the sentence has more body. Take any energy statement and put feet flat on the floor before saying the release; the release has more ground.

When a practice script names a specific anchor, use that one. When it doesn't, hand on heart is the default. If a hand-on-heart placement is uncomfortable (clothing, location, day), the hand on the belly is the next choice.

Embodiment as practice draws on somatic therapy traditions (Peter Levine's Somatic Experiencing and Pat Ogden's Sensorimotor Psychotherapy), both built on the observation that the body holds patterns that talk-only work cannot reach. It also draws on polyvagal theory (Stephen Porges, 1994 onward), which gives the framework for how specific postures shift the nervous system between safety, mobilisation, and shutdown. The pairing of a specific posture with a spoken sentence is documented across these traditions. The practical anchors used in this library (hand on heart, hand on belly, palms up, both hands at the heart) are drawn from across Rebecca J Page's MONEY (2025) and MANIFESTING (2025) programs.
